edges.quote: Meanwhile, the smallfolk of Fair Isle and such knights as still remained on the island rose up in red rebellion.
edges.note: Dalton Greyjoy's murder dissolves the ironborn fleet massed against him, opening the door for Fair Isle's smallfolk to rise.
edges.rationale: Text explicitly narrates the mechanism (line 93: the assembled fleet 'began to dissolve, as captain after captain slipped away for home' once word of Dalton's death spread) immediately before the rebellion. The uprising itself is a free collective decision by the smallfolk/remaining knights, not a forced mechanical consequence, so ENABLES (precondition removes the occupying threat) rather than CAUSES preserves their agency.

edges.quote: A sudden storm blew up as the fleet was making its way past the drylands west of Salt Shore, scattering the ships and sinking two.
edges.note: Wires the zero-edge stub: Alyn Velaryon commanded the fleet that lost two ships to this storm off Dorne.
edges.rationale: No causal antecedent or consequence is narrated for the storm itself (pure weather, no textual causal claim per hard rule 4), so a role edge is the honest wiring rather than forcing a causal type.

edges.quote: until the latter slew the former in a quarrel over Lord Farman’s daughter Lysa
edges.note: Gunthor Goodbrother's killing of Alester Wynch is the final beat of the Faircastle holdout inside the wider Fair Isle rebellion.
edges.rationale: Text places this explicitly as the resolution of the last ironborn holdout (Faircastle) within the same rebellion narrated at line 97 -- a beat inside a named event hub, not an independent causal chain.

edges.quote: Instead he whispered something in the admiral's ear, a secret that changed the course of Westerosi history.
edges.note: Drazenko Rogare's whispered secret at Sunspear is what sends Oakenfist to Lys to negotiate Viserys's ransom.
edges.rationale: A human decision (Oakenfist's choice to sail for Lys and then agree to Lysandro's terms) mediates the link, so MOTIVATES targeting the downstream decision-event (the ransom pact itself) is used instead of CAUSES, per the agency-collapse check.

edges.quote: The chance encounter between Alyn Velaryon and Drazenko Rogare at Sunspear had provided a perfect opportunity to effect the return of Prince Viserys to his brother
edges.note: Viserys's years-old captivity is the underlying condition that makes a ransom negotiation necessary at all.
edges.rationale: Marquee-spine check requested: links the 130 AC capture to the 134 AC ransom pact. Oakenfist's specific decision to travel to Lys and accept 'ruinous terms' is a free choice (explicitly editorialized by Mushroom as a mistake), so MOTIVATES on the decision-event, not CAUSES.

edges.quote: a dragon in the skies above the city
edges.note: Rhaena's first flight on Morning is one of the two 'momentous events' the text credits for the season's joy.
edges.rationale: The lysene-spring node's own Origins prose names Rhaena flying Morning as one of the two events the Seven Kingdoms rejoiced at during the Spring -- a beat inside that named event hub, not an independent chain.

edges.quote: Thereafter it was Aegon who was silent, for his old gloom had settled over him once again, and he seemed to lose all interest in his court and kingdom.
edges.note: Gaemon's poisoning-death drives Aegon III's relapse into gloom and withdrawal from court.
edges.rationale: Disposition change in a character, not a new discrete event -- the character-target form of MOTIVATES fits per the vocabulary definition.

edges.quote: On the morning of the twelfth day of the secret siege, Thaddeus Rowan was brought forth in chains to
edges.note: Marston Waters's regime tortures Rowan into a confession specifically to break the twelve-day standoff at Maegor's Holdfast.
edges.rationale: Marston/Graceford's decision to extract a confession is a deliberate human choice made under the pressure of the ongoing siege, not an automatic mechanical result -- MOTIVATES targeting the decision-event (per the preferred event-target form) rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: The traitors here are those who tortured him to bring forth this false confession.
edges.note: Rowan's coerced false confession, unraveled under Viserys's questioning, provokes the king's counterstroke: declaring Rowan innocent and ordering Graceford's arrest.
edges.rationale: The king's counterstroke is his own free decisive act in response to the exposed torture -- character-target MOTIVATES (per row 193's requested grounding) rather than collapsing his agency into CAUSES.

edges.quote: Yet as Ser Marston grasped it, Ser Mervyn seized his wrist, drew a dagger with his other hand, and plunged it into Waters's belly.
edges.note: Row 194: the exposure of the false confession leads Graceford to name Mervyn Flowers as a conspirator; Marston's attempt to arrest his sworn brother ends with Flowers stabbing him.
edges.rationale: A chain of individual decisions mediates this (Graceford naming names under threat, Marston choosing to confront Flowers personally, Flowers choosing to kill him) -- MOTIVATES on the downstream decision/killing-event rather than CAUSES, per the agency-collapse check.

edges.quote: Yet as Ser Marston grasped it, Ser Mervyn seized his wrist, drew a dagger with his other hand, and plunged it into Waters's belly.
edges.note: Role edge wiring the new death-of-marston-waters node: Mervyn Flowers is the killer.
edges.rationale: Standard AGENT_IN role edge for the newly minted event node.

edges.quote: Yet as Ser Marston grasped it, Ser Mervyn seized his wrist, drew a dagger with his other hand, and plunged it into Waters's belly.
edges.note: Role edge wiring the new death-of-marston-waters node: Marston Waters is the victim.
edges.rationale: Standard VICTIM_IN role edge for the newly minted event node.

edges.quote: King Aegon never warmed to him, it is true, but His Grace did not have a trusting nature, and the events of the past year had only served to deepen his suspicions.
edges.note: Row 204: the trauma and betrayal of the Rowan conspiracy year is what the text names as deepening Aegon's suspicion, which shapes his cold dismissal of the regents and Hand the instant he comes of age.
edges.rationale: Explicit textual grounding (not year-only adjacency, per hard rule 4) tying the conspiracy year to Aegon's mistrustful disposition toward regents/Hand; MOTIVATES on the specific downstream decision-event (the dismissal scene, newly minted since no node existed for this marquee beat distinct from the whole-period regency-of-aegon-iii container).

edges.quote: made an enemy of a man who had been amongst his most leal and devoted servants.
edges.note: Row 206: Aegon's curt dismissal turns Torrhen Manderly, a loyal servant, into an enemy who never forgives the king.
edges.rationale: Disposition change in a character (Manderly's turn against the king) -- character-target MOTIVATES.

edges.quote: “I shall,” King Aegon said. “You are sitting in my chair.”
edges.note: Role edge wiring the new aegon-iii-dismisses-the-regents-and-hand node: Aegon III is the agent of the dismissal.
edges.rationale: Standard AGENT_IN role edge for the newly minted event node; also grounds the row 204 seed's exact quoted line.

edges.quote: You have served me well, no doubt, as you served my mother before me, but it was my lords who chose you. You may return to White Harbor.
edges.note: Role edge wiring the new node: Torrhen Manderly is the Hand dismissed alongside the three lot-chosen regents.
edges.rationale: Standard role edge for the newly minted event node.

edges.quote: For the rest of that year, the Grand Maester served as both regent and Hand, for the realm required governance and Aegon had still not reached the age of manhood.
edges.note: Wires the zero-edge gathering-of-the-lords stub: Rowan's death leaves the regency vacant, and Munkun's solo caretaker rule leads directly into the summons that produces the 136 AC gathering.
edges.rationale: The vacancy is a precondition; the lords' answering of 'the king's summons' (a free choice by many independent third parties) actually produces the gathering, so ENABLES preserves that collective agency rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: Finally, in despair at reaching an agreement, Grand Maester Munkun proposed that three regents be chosen by lot.
edges.note: The assembled lords' failure to agree on new regents is what opens the door to Munkun's lot-selection proposal.
edges.rationale: Munkun's proposal (seconded by Viserys) is a specific free decision by named actors responding to the gathering's deadlock -- ENABLES, not CAUSES.

edges.quote: Instead the lords turned to the northman, Torrhen Manderly, Lord of White Harbor
edges.note: The same assembly that could not agree on regents also selects the new King's Hand.
edges.rationale: The lords' choice of Manderly (after rejecting Peake, Velaryon, Tully, and Blackwood as candidates) is their own free deliberation -- ENABLES.

edges.quote: The news of this monstrous dragonbirth and its bloody aftermath were greatly troubling to King Aegon, and soon led to angry words between His Grace and his brother.
edges.note: The monstrous hatchling that maims Laena directly and immediately sparks Aegon's ban on dragon eggs and the quarrel with Viserys. [S204 adjudication: TRIGGERS->CAUSES — Aegon's egg-ban decision mediates]
edges.rationale: Text states the second event followed 'soon' as a direct emotional reaction with no deliberation between -- an immediate spark, not a considered third-party decision, so TRIGGERS fits rather than ENABLES/MOTIVATES.

edges.quote: Lysaro was sentenced to be chained naked to a pillar before the Temple of Trade, where all those despoiled by him would be allowed to whip him
edges.note: Lysaro's capture and return to Lys makes his trial and eventual public scourging possible.
edges.rationale: Capture is a precondition; the specific brutal sentence is a free judicial decision by the Lys magisters (contrast his siblings' lighter, separately-deliberated punishments), so ENABLES rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: Daeron conquered Dorne, but was unable to hold it, and died young.
edges.note: Row 7: fills a gap -- no role edge previously linked Daeron I to the Conquest of Dorne event node he is credited with.
edges.rationale: Honest, well-grounded role edge. See flags for why the fuller causal claim (failure to hold Dorne -> early death) is NOT typed here.

new_nodes.name: Death of Dalton Greyjoy
new_nodes.identity: Lord Dalton Greyjoy, the Red Kraken, is slain in his bed at Faircastle by his salt wife Tess, who cuts his throat with his own dagger and then drowns herself; his death instantly dissolves the ironborn fleet massed against Alyn Oakenfist and opens a succession crisis on the Iron Islands.

new_nodes.name: Death of Marston Waters
new_nodes.identity: Ser Marston Waters, Lord Commander of the Kingsguard and King's Hand, is fatally stabbed in the belly by his sworn brother Ser Mervyn Flowers when he goes to arrest him as a conspirator in the plot against King Aegon III; he dies of the wound that same night.

new_nodes.name: Aegon III dismisses the regents and Hand
new_nodes.identity: On his sixteenth nameday, King Aegon III enters the small council chamber, declares the regency at an end, dismisses lot-chosen regents Stackspear, Merryweather, and Grandison, and curtly sends Hand of the King Torrhen Manderly back to White Harbor, taking the governance of the Seven Kingdoms into his own hands.

flags: Seed row 183 (fab-the-lysene-spring-24-p01, line 239 'names the regent and the regent names the Hand, and round and round and round we dance'): judged commentary only. It is Prince Viserys's defiant, mocking rhetoric during the drawbridge standoff, not a narrated causal claim about the regency structure -- deliberately NOT forced into an edge, per the assignment's instruction.

Seed row 7 (fab-lineages-and-family-tree-25, line 41 'Daeron conquered Dorne, but was unable to hold it, and died young'): this unit is a thin bracketed succession-list annotation with no elaboration of a death mechanism (no assassination, wound, or rebellion detail given here). Wired only the honest role edge (daeron-i-targaryen AGENT_IN conquest-of-dorne, J-E22); did NOT type a causal edge from 'unable to hold Dorne' to 'died young' since the grounding in this specific unit is too thin (no textual mechanism asserted, and rule 4 bars year/sequence-only causation). Fuller grounding for Daeron I's death likely exists in other F&B/TWOIAF material outside this unit's scope.

fab-lineages-and-family-tree-25.md is mostly non-narrative: a 17-monarch succession list (matches the S203 finding that the family-tree diagrams were raster images, never text) followed by a full transcript of a 2019 GRRM/Dan Jones stage interview about the writing of Fire & Blood. No further causal-spine material exists in this unit beyond the row 7 seed.

harvest.note: Extensive physical/character description of Racallio Ryndoon (six-and-a-half feet tall, purple dyed hair and beard, rolling gait) -- vivid homeless-quote candidate.

'The Hand was in a black state even before the poisoning, for he had only recently suffered his own personal tragedy when his young wife, the Lady Floris, died in childbirth.' -- Thaddeus Rowan backstory not otherwise captured.
Lyonel Hightower's welcome of Oakenfist and Lady Sam at Oldtown (beacon-guided harbor entry, Citadel access, Starry Sept blessing) -- hospitality/food-and-welcome theme.
The Mermaid pillow house description (parrots from the Summer Islands, monkeys from Sothoryos, rumored mermaid) -- vivid worldbuilding not elsewhere captured.

Sheepstealer and Nettles's last recorded sighting in Westerosi annals (a 'ragged woman clinging to its back' fleeing into the mountains after Robert Rowan's host rouses the dragon) -- major lore beat, check whether already wired to Sheepstealer/Nettles nodes.
Trial-by-combat between Gareth Long and Victor Risley ('You always were a bloody fool, Victor') -- vivid scene not otherwise captured in the causal spine.
